content: Which of the following substances has the highest elasticity?
explanation:
Elasticity is the ability of an object to return to its original shape after being deformed. Steel has the highest elasticity of the given substances. Copper is a ductile metal that can be easily bent and shaped. Rubber is an elastic material that can be stretched and compressed. Sponge is a porous material that absorbs water and other liquids.

Steel is an alloy of iron and carbon that is known for its strength, toughness, and elasticity. Steel is used in a wide variety of applications, including construction, manufacturing, and transportation.

content: A wire is stretched under a force. If the wire suddenly snaps the temperature of the wire
explanation: Work done on the wire to strain it will be stored as energy which is converted to heat. Therefore the temperature increases.

content: A beaker of radius 15 cm is filled with a liquid of surface tension 0.075 N/m. Force across an imaginary diameter on the surface of the liquid is
explanation:
Surface tension = 0.075 N/m;

Diameter = 30 cm = 0.30 m.

$$\therefore$$ Force = 0.075 $$\times$$ 0.30 = 0.0225 N = 2.25 $$\times$$ 10$$-$$2 N.

content: A piece of wood is floating in water. When the temperature of water rises, the apparent weight of the wood will
explanation:
To determine the effect of a temperature rise in water on the apparent weight of a piece of wood floating in it, let's first understand the relevant principles involved. The apparent weight of an object submerged in a fluid is determined by the buoyant force exerted on the object by the fluid. According to Archimedes' principle, the buoyant force is equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the object.

When the temperature of the water rises, two main factors come into play:

1. Density of Water: As the temperature of water increases, its density decreases. This is because water expands when heated, reducing its mass per unit volume.

2. Buoyant Force: Since the buoyant force is directly proportional to the density of the fluid (water) and the volume of the displaced fluid, a decrease in the density of water will result in a decrease in the buoyant force.

The apparent weight of an object is given by the real weight of the object minus the buoyant force:

$$W_{\text{apparent}} = W_{\text{real}} - F_{\text{buoyant}}$$

As the buoyant force decreases with the decrease in water's density, the apparent weight of the wood will increase. This can be verified by the following equation:

$$\Delta W_{\text{apparent}} = - \Delta F_{\text{buoyant}}$$

Since $$\Delta F_{\text{buoyant}}$$ is negative when the density of water decreases, overall, the apparent weight $$W_{\text{apparent}}$$ increases.

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option A: increase

content: One end of a stecl wire is fixed to the ceiling of an elevator moving up with an acceleration $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ and a load of 10 kg hangs from the other end. If the cross section of the wire is $2 \mathrm{~cm}^2$, then the longitudinal strain in the wire will be ( $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ and $\mathrm{Y}=2.0 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$ )
explanation:
From the equation of motion

$$ \begin{aligned} & \quad \begin{aligned} T-m g & =m a \\ & \\ \Rightarrow& =m g+m a=m(g+a) \\ & =10(10+2)=120 \mathrm{~N} \end{aligned}\\ &\therefore \text { Young's modulus, } \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{gathered} Y=\frac{T / A}{\frac{\Delta l}{l}} \\ \Rightarrow \frac{\Delta l}{l}=\frac{T}{A Y}=\frac{120}{2 \times 10^{-4} \times 2 \times 10^{11}}=3 \times 10^{-6} \end{gathered} $$

content: Three different liquids are filled in a U-tube as shown in figure. Their densities are $\rho_1, \rho_2$ and $\rho_3$ respectively. From the figure we may conclude that
explanation:
Pressure on left side,

$$ p_1=p_{\mathrm{atm}}+\rho_1 h g+\rho_3 \frac{h}{2} g $$

Pressure on right side,

$$ p_2=p_{\mathrm{atm}}+\rho_2 h g $$

Equating the pressures,

$$ \begin{array}{rlrl} p_1 & =p_2 \\ p_{\mathrm{atm}}+\rho_1 h g+\rho_3 \frac{h}{2} g & =p_{\mathrm{atm}}+\rho_2 h g \\ \Rightarrow \rho_3 & =2\left(\rho_2-\rho_1\right) \end{array} $$

content: A piece of granite floats at the interface of mercury and water contained in a beaker as in figure. If the densities of granite, water and mercury are $\rho, \rho_1$ and $\rho_2$ respectively, the ratio of the volume of granite in water to the volume of granite in mercury is
explanation:
$$ F=F_w+F_m $$

$$ \begin{aligned}Weight\,\, of \,\,granite,\,\, W & =\rho g V \\ F_w & =\rho_1 g V_w \\and\,\, F_m & =\rho_2 g V_m \end{aligned} $$

Since, the granite is floating,

$$ \rho g V=\rho_1 g V_W+\rho_2 g V_m \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

and also,

$$ \begin{aligned} V & =V_m+V_W \\ V_W & =V-V_m \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} $$

Using Eq. (i) and (ii)

$$ V_m=\frac{\left(\rho-\rho_1\right) V}{\left(\rho_2-\rho_1\right)}, V_w=\frac{\left(\rho_2-\rho\right) V}{\left(\rho_2-\rho_1\right)} $$

Now,

$$ \frac{V_w}{V_m}=\frac{\left(\rho_2-\rho\right)}{\left(\rho-\rho_1\right)} $$

content: The apparent coefficient of expansion of a liquid, when heated in a copper vessel is $C$ and when heated in silver vessel is $S$. If $A$ is linear coefficient of expansion of copper, then linear coefficient of expansion of silver is
explanation:
Let β be the true volume‐expansion coefficient of the liquid, A the linear coefficient of copper, and let B be the linear coefficient of silver. Then the vessel’s volume‐expansion coefficients are 3A (copper) and 3B (silver), and the apparent expansions are

$$C = β - 3A,\quad S = β - 3B.$$

Eliminating β,

$$β = C + 3A = S + 3B\;\Longrightarrow\;3B = C + 3A - S\;\Longrightarrow\;B = \frac{C + 3A - S}{3}.$$

So the linear coefficient of expansion of silver is $\displaystyle \frac{C + 3A - S}{3}\,$.

content: The elastic potential energy of a strained body is
explanation:
The elastic potential energy stored in a strained body can be derived from the relationship between stress, strain, and the deformation energy per unit volume. This relationship is often represented using the formula for the elastic potential energy density in a material. The correct option is:

Option D: $$\frac{1}{2} \times \text{stress} \times \text{strain} \times \text{volume}$$

Explanation:

Elastic potential energy is the energy stored in an object when it is deformed but not permanently. When a material follows Hooke's Law, the stress (force per unit area) and strain (deformation per unit length) are linearly related. The formula for elastic potential energy $$U$$ in a body is given by:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{stress} \times \text{strain} \times \text{volume}$$

Where:

- Stress is the internal force per unit area within the material.

  • Strain is the relative deformation, i.e., the change in length divided by the original length.

  • Volume is the measure of the space occupied by the body where the deformation occurs.

The factor $$\frac{1}{2}$$ comes from the integration of the linear stress-strain relationship, considering that stress increases linearly with strain from 0 to its maximum value. Thus, the correct representation of the elastic potential energy is given by Option D.

content: A body floats with $$\frac{1}{n}$$ of its volume keeping outside of water. If the body has been taken to height $$\mathrm{h}$$ inside water and released, it will come to the surface after time t. Then
explanation:
$\begin{aligned} & V d g=V\left(1-\frac{1}{n}\right) \rho g \\\\ & d=\left(\frac{n-1}{n}\right) \rho \\\\ & a=\frac{V \rho g-V d g}{V d}=\left(\frac{\rho}{d}-1\right) g=\left(\frac{n}{n-1}-1\right) g=\frac{g}{h-1}\end{aligned}$

$h=\frac{1}{2} a t^2, \quad t=\sqrt{\frac{2 h}{a}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 h(n-1)}{g}}$

$\therefore t \propto \sqrt{n-1}$

content: Water is filled in a cylindrical vessel of height $$\mathrm{H}$$. A hole is made at height $$\mathrm{z}$$ from the bottom, as shown in the figure. The value of z for which the range (R) of the emerging water through the hole will be maximum for
explanation:
Here we will use the Bernoulli's equation, and the concept of horizontal projectile motion.

By Bernoulli's equation at point (1) and (2),

$${P_1} + {1 \over 2}\rho v_1^2 + \rho g{H_1} = {P_2} + {1 \over 2}\rho v_2^2 + \rho g{H_2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {p_{atm}} + 0 + \rho gH = {p_{atm}} + {1 \over 2}\rho {v^2} + \rho gz$$

$$ \Rightarrow {v^2} = 2g(H - z)$$ .... (1)

By projectile motion,

$$R = v \times t \Rightarrow t = {R \over v}$$

$$ - z = - {1 \over 2}g{t^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow z = {1 \over 2}g{{{R^2}} \over {{v^2}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {R^2} = {{2'z{v^2}} \over g}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {R^2} = {{2z \times 2g(H - z)} \over { - g}}$$ [from eq. (i)]

$$ \Rightarrow {R^2} = 4z(H - z)$$

By taking derivative with respect to z,

$$ \Rightarrow 2R{{dR} \over {dz}} = 4z( - 1) + (H - z)4$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{dR} \over {dz}} = {{4H - 8z} \over {2R}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{dR} \over {dz}} = 0 \Rightarrow 4H - 8z = 0 \Rightarrow z = {H \over 2}$$

content: A metal plate of area $$10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}^2$$ rests on a layer of castor oil, $$2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}$$ thick, whose coefficient of viscosity is $$1.55 \mathrm{~Ns} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$$. The approximate horizontal force required to move the plate with a uniform speed of $$3 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$$ is
explanation:
To determine the horizontal force required to move the metal plate with a uniform speed, we can use the formula for viscous force, which is given by:

$$ F = \eta \cdot A \cdot \frac{v}{d} $$

where:

  • $$\eta$$ is the coefficient of viscosity (given as $$1.55 \mathrm{~Ns} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$$).
  • $$A$$ is the area of the metal plate (given as $$10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}^2$$).
  • $$v$$ is the uniform speed of the plate (given as $$3 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$$).
  • $$d$$ is the thickness of the castor oil layer (given as $$2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}$$).

Substituting the given values into the formula, we get:

$$ F = 1.55 \cdot 10^{-2} \cdot \frac{3 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} $$

First, handle the fraction:

$$ \frac{3 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{3}{2} \times 10^{1} = 1.5 \times 10 $$

Now, substitute this back into the equation for the force:

$$ F = 1.55 \cdot 10^{-2} \cdot 15 = 1.55 \cdot 0.15 $$

Finally, calculate the force:

$$ F = 0.2325 \mathrm{~N} $$

Therefore, the approximate horizontal force required to move the plate with a uniform speed is:

Option B: 0.2325 N

content: As shown in the figure, a liquid is at same levels in two arms of a U-tube of uniform cross-section when at rest. If the U-tube moves with an acceleration 'f' towards right, the difference between liquid heights between two arms of the U-tube will be, (acceleration due to gravity = g)
explanation:
The FBD of the liquid above the level is

$$\therefore \quad \tan \theta=\frac{f}{g}=\frac{h}{a} \therefore h=\frac{f}{g} a$$

content: As shown in the figure, a pump is designed as horizontal cylinder with a piston having area A and an outlet orifice having an area 'a'. The piston moves with a constant velocity under the action of force F. If the density of the liquid is $$\rho$$, then the speed of the liquid emerging from the orifice is, (assume $$\mathrm{A} > >$$ a)
explanation:
Let $$u$$, and $$v$$ are the speed at inlet and outlet of the pump.

$$\therefore$$ Applying Bernoulli's theorem at points 1 and 2.

$$p_0+\frac{F}{A}+\frac{1}{2} \rho u^2=p_0+\frac{1}{2} \rho v^2$$

$$\therefore \quad \frac{F}{A}=\frac{1}{2} \rho\left(v^2-u^2\right)$$ ..... (i)

By continuity equation, $$A u=a v$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad u=\frac{a v}{A}$$ ..... (ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{F}{A}=\frac{1}{2} \rho\left[v^2-\frac{a^2 v^2}{A^2}\right] & =\frac{1}{2} \rho v^2\left(\frac{A^2-a^2}{A^2}\right) \\ \text { As, } A \gg a \Rightarrow \frac{A^2-a^2}{A^2} & =1, \\ v & =\sqrt{2 F / \rho A} \end{aligned}$$

content: 27 drops of mercury coalesce to form a bigger drop. What is the relative increase in surface energy?
explanation:
Let $$r$$ be the radius of small mercury drop.

Thus, total volume of 27 drops $$ = 27 \times {4 \over 3}\,\pi {r^3}$$

Total volume of bigger drop $$ = {4 \over 3}\,\pi {R^3}$$

$$\therefore$$ According to given situation,

$${4 \over 3}\,\pi {R^3} = 27 \times {4 \over 3}\,\pi {r^3}$$

$$ \Rightarrow R = 3r$$

Now, surface energy = surface tension $$\times$$ area

$$ = T \times A$$

Surface energy of 27 drops $$ = T \times 27 \times (4\pi {r^2})$$

$$ = 27 \times 4\pi {r^2}T$$

Surface energy of bigger drop $$ = T \times 4\pi {R^2}$$

$$ = T \times 4\pi {(3r)^2}$$

$$ = 9 \times 4\pi {r^2}T$$

Increase in surface energy $$ = 9 \times 4\pi {r^2}T - 27 \times 4\pi {r^2}T$$

$$ = - 18 \times 4\pi {r^2}T$$

$$\therefore$$ Relative increase in surface energy

$$ = {{ - 18 \times 4\pi {r^2}T} \over {27 \times 4\pi {r^2}T}} = - {2 \over 3}$$

content: A body of density 1.2 $$\times$$ 10<sup>3</sup> kg/m<sup>3</sup> is dropped from rest from a height 1 m into a liquid to density 2.4 $$\times$$ 10<sup>3</sup> kg/m<sup>3</sup>. Neglecting all dissipative effects, the maximum depth to which the body sinks before returning to float on the surface is
explanation:
Given, density of body = 1.2 $$\times$$ 103 kg/m3 = $$\rho$$b

Density of liquid = 2.4 $$\times$$ 103 kg/m3 = $$\rho$$t

Height of fall = 1 m = H

The the volume of the body be V.

Then, buoyant force acting on the body when it is totally immersed in liquid = ($$\rho$$lVg)

Weight at the body = ($$\rho$$bVg)

Net upward force acting on the body = ($$\rho$$l $$-$$ $$\rho$$b) Vg

Net deceleration produced

$$(a) = {{Net\,upward\,force} \over {Mass\,of\,body}}$$

$$(a) = {{({\rho _l} - {\rho _b})Vg} \over {{\rho _b}V}}$$

$$(a) = {{({\rho _l} - {\rho _b})} \over {{\rho _b}}}g$$

Let initial velocity of the body be u, then

$${1 \over 2}({\rho _b}V){u^2} = {\rho _b}VgH$$

$$ \Rightarrow {u^2} = 2gH$$

Final velocity of the body will be zero.

$$ \Rightarrow {v^2} - {u^2} = 2as$$

$$ \Rightarrow {(0)^2} - 2gH = - 2{{({\rho _l} - {\rho _b})g} \over {{\rho _b}}} \times s$$

$$ \Rightarrow s = {{{\rho _b}} \over {{\rho _l} - {\rho _b}}}\,.\,H = \left( {{{1.2 \times {{10}^3}} \over {2.4 \times {{10}^3} - 1.2 \times {{10}^3}}}} \right)\,.\,1$$

$$ = {{1.2} \over {1.2}} = 1$$ m

content: Two solid spheres S<sub>1</sub> and S<sub>2</sub> of same uniform density fall from rest under gravity in a viscous medium and after sometime, reach terminal velocities v<sub>1</sub> and v<sub>2</sub>, respectively. If ratio of masses $${{{m_1}} \over {{m_2}}} = 8$$, then $${{{v_1}} \over {{v_2}}}$$ will be equal to
explanation:
Given densities of both spheres are same. Let it be $$\rho$$ unit and density of liquid be $$\sigma$$ and $$\eta $$ be its viscosity.

Let radius of sphere S1 be r1 and that of sphere S2 be r2 and volumes are V1 and V2, respectively.

Now,

$${{{m_1}} \over {{V_1}}} = {{{m_2}} \over {{V_2}}}$$ ($$\because$$ density is same)

$$ \Rightarrow {{{m_1}} \over {{4 \over 3}\pi r_1^3}} = {{{m_2}} \over {{4 \over 3}\pi r_2^3}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\left( {{{{r_1}} \over {{r_2}}}} \right)^3} = \left( {{{{m_1}} \over {{m_2}}}} \right) = {8 \over 1}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{{r_1}} \over {{r_2}}} = {2 \over 1}$$

Now, terminal velocity is given by

$$v = {{2{r^2}(\rho - \sigma )g} \over {9\eta }}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{{v_1}} \over {{v_2}}} = {{{{2r_1^2(\rho - \sigma )g} \over {9\eta }}} \over {{{2r_2^2(\rho - \sigma )g} \over {9\eta }}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{{v_1}} \over {{v_2}}} = {\left( {{{{r_1}} \over {{r_2}}}} \right)^2} = {2^2} = 4$$

content: Under isothermal conditions, two soap bubbles of radii a and b coalesce to form a single bubble of radius c. If the external pressure is p, then surface tension of the bubbles is
explanation:
Under isothermal conditions,

$${p_1}{V_1} + {p_2}{V_2} = pV$$

$$ \Rightarrow \left( {p - {{4T} \over a}} \right)\,.\,{4 \over 3}\pi {a^3} + \left( {p - {{4T} \over b}} \right)\,.\,{4 \over 3}\pi {b^3} = \left( {p - {{4T} \over c}} \right)\,.\,{4 \over 3}\pi {c^3}$$

where, T is surface tension of soap bubble.

$$ \Rightarrow p[{c^3} - ({a^3} + {b^3})] - 4T({a^2} + {b^2} - {c^2}) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow T = {{p({c^3} - {a^3} - {b^3})} \over {4({a^2} + {b^2} - {c^2})}}$$

content:
A steel and a brass wire, each of length 50 cm and cross-sectional area 0.005 cm2 hang from a ceiling and are 15 cm apart. Lower ends of the wires are attached to a light horizontal bar. A suitable downward load is applied to the bar, so that each of the wires extends in length by 0.1 cm. At what distance from the steel wire, the load must be applied?

[Young's modulus of steel = 2 $$ \times $$ 1012 dyne/cm2 and that of brass = 1 $$ \times $$ 1012 dyne/cm2]
explanation:
Let x be the distance from steel wire, where load is applied as shown in the figure below.


Given, Young's modulus of steel,

Ysteel = 2 $$ \times $$ 1012 dyne/cm2

Young's modulus of brass, Ybrass = 1 $$ \times $$ 1012 dyne/cm2

Cross-sectional area of wires, A = 0.005 cm2

Extension in wires, $$\Delta $$l = 0.1 cm

We know that,

Young's modulus, $${Y_{steel}} = {{stress} \over {strain}} = {{{F_s}/A} \over {{{\Delta l} \over l}}} = {{{F_s}.l} \over {\Delta lA}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${F_s} = {Y_{steel}} \times {{\Delta l} \over l} \times A$$

= $$2 \times {10^{12}} \times {{0.1} \over {50}} \times 0.005$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ Fs = 2 $$ \times $$ 107 dyne

Similarly,

$${F_8} = {Y_{brass}} \times {{\Delta l} \over l} \times A$$

= $$1 \times {10^{12}} \times {{0.1} \over {50}} \times 0.005$$

= 107 dyne

From given figure,

Fs $$ \times $$ x = FB $$ \times $$ (15 $$ - $$ x)

$$ \Rightarrow $$ 2 $$ \times $$ 107 $$ \times $$ x = 107 $$ \times $$ (15 $$ - $$ x)

$$ \Rightarrow $$2x = 15 $$ - $$ x

$$ \Rightarrow $$ 3x = 15 $$ \Rightarrow $$ x = 5 cm

content:
Which of the following diagrams correctly shows the relation between the terminal velocity v<sub>T</sub> of a spherical body falling in a liquid and viscosity $$\eta $$
 of the liquid?
explanation:
We know that,

terminal velocity, $${v_T} = {{2{r^2}} \over {9\eta }}(\rho s - \rho L)g$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$$${v_T} \propto {1 \over \eta }$$ , where $$\eta $$ is viscosity of the liquid.

Hence, graph between terminal velocity vT and viscosity of liquid $$\eta $$ is as shown below.

content:
A compressive force is applied to a uniform rod of rectangular cross-section so that its length decreases by 1%. If the Poisson's ratio for the material of the rod be 0.2, which of the following statements is correct?

"The volume approximately ................."
explanation:
Given, decrement in the length = 1%

Poisson's ratio for material of the rod, $$\sigma$$ = 0.2

As we know that,

Volume = v = $$\pi$$r2l, [where, l is the length of the rod]

$${{\Delta V} \over V} = {{2\Delta r} \over r} + {{\Delta l} \over l}$$ ...(i)

Since, Poisson's ratio, $$\sigma = {{ - \Delta D/D} \over {\Delta L/L}}$$

$$ = {{ - \Delta r/r} \over {\Delta L/L}}$$

So, Eq. (i) can be written as,

$${{\Delta V} \over V} = {{\Delta l} \over l}(1 - 2\sigma )$$

$${{\Delta V} \over V} \times 100 = \left( {{{\Delta l} \over l} \times 100} \right) \times (1 - 2\sigma )$$

$$ = - 1 \times [1 - 2 \times (0.2)]$$

$$ = - 1 \times [1 - 0.4]$$

$$ = - 0.6\% $$

Here, negative sign shows the decrement in the volume.

content: A small spherical body of radius r and density $$\rho $$ moves with the terminal velocity v in a fluid of coefficient of viscosity $$\eta $$ and density $$\sigma$$. What will be the net force on the body?
explanation:
When the spherical body falls with constant velocity, i.e. terminal velocity then the net force becomes zero, i.e. the weight of the body is equal to the buoyancy force.

Hence Fnet = 0

content: The stress along the length of a rod (with rectangular cross-section) is 1% of the Young's modulus of its material. What is the approximate percentage of change of its volume? (Poisson's ratio of the material of the rod is 0.3).
explanation:
$$ \because $$ Stress, $${F \over {\Delta A}} = 1\% $$ of $$Y = {Y \over {100}}$$

But Young's modulus, $$Y = {{stress} \over {strain}} = {{{F \over {\Delta A}}} \over {{{\Delta l} \over l}}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$Y = {{{Y \over {100}}} \over {{{\Delta l} \over l}}}$$ (putting $${F \over {\Delta A}} = {Y \over {100}}$$)

$$ \therefore $$ $${{\Delta l} \over l} = {1 \over {100}}$$

Poisson's ratio, $$\sigma = {{{{\Delta r} \over r}} \over {{{\Delta l} \over l}}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${{\Delta r} \over r} = - \sigma \,.\,{{\Delta l} \over l} = {{ - 0.3} \over {100}}$$

$${{\Delta r} \over r} = {{ - 0.3} \over {100}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ Change in volume, $${{\Delta V} \over V} = {{2\Delta r} \over r} + {{\Delta l} \over l}$$

$$ = {{2 \times ( - 0.3)} \over {100}} + {1 \over {100}}$$

$$ = {{1 - 0.6} \over {100}} = {{0.4} \over {100}}$$

$$\Delta V\% = 0.4\% $$

content: What will be the approximate terminal velocity of a rain drop of diameter $${1.8 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}$$ m, when density of rain water $$ \approx $$ $${1.8 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}$$ N-sm<sup>$$-$$2</sup> (Neglect buoyancy of air)
explanation:
Terminal velocity, $$v = {2 \over 9}{r^2}{{(\rho - \sigma )} \over \eta }g$$

Neglecting buoyancy effect of the fluid,

$$v = {2 \over 9}\,.\,{\rho \over \eta }{r^2}g$$

Putting the given values, we get,

$$v = {2 \over 9} \times {{{{10}^3} \times {{(0.9 \times {{10}^{ - 3}})}^2}} \over {1.8 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}} \times 9.8$$ = 98 ms$$-$$1

content: A uniform capillary tube of length l and inner radius r with its upper end sealed is submerged vertically into water. The outside pressure is p<sub>0</sub> and surface tension of water is $$\gamma$$. When a length x of the capillary is submerged into water, it is found that water levels inside and outside the capillary coincide. The value of x is
explanation:
When the sealed capillary tube is submerged vertically into water, the pressure inside the tube changes

For the inside capillary,

$${p_1}{V_1} = {p_2}{V_2}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${p_0}(lA) = p'(l - x)A$$

where, p' is pressure in capillary after being submerged

$$ \therefore $$ $$p' = {{{p_0}l} \over {l - x}}$$

According to question, since level of water inside capillary coincides with outside.

$$ \therefore $$ $$p' - {p_0} = {{2\gamma } \over r}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${{{p_0}l} \over {l - x}} - {p_0} = {{2\gamma } \over r} = {l \over {\left( {1 + {{{p_0}r} \over {2\gamma }}} \right)}}$$

content: A liquid of bulk modulus k is compressed by applying an external pressure such that its density increases by 0.01%. The pressure applied on the liquid is
explanation:
The Bulk modulus k

$$k = - {p \over {\Delta V/|V}}$$ .... (i)

where, p = Pressure

$$\Delta$$V = Change in volume

V = Volume of liquid

From (i)

$${p \over k} = - {{\Delta V} \over V} = {{\Delta p} \over p}$$

$$ \Rightarrow p = {{k\Delta p} \over p}$$

$$\Delta p = 0.01\% = 0.01/100$$

$$p = {k \over {10000}}$$

content: A solid rectangular sheet has two different coefficients of linear expansion $$\alpha$$<sub>1</sub> and $$\alpha$$<sub>2</sub> along its length and breadth respectively. The coefficient of surface expansion is (for $$\alpha$$<sub>1</sub> t << 1, $$\alpha$$<sub>2</sub>t < <1)
explanation:
The coefficient of linear expansion along its length = $$\alpha$$1


The coefficient of linear expansion along its breadth = $$\alpha$$2

Increase in length,

$${L_t} = {l_0}(1 + {\alpha _1}\Delta t)$$

Increase in breadth,

$${B_t} = {b_0}(1 + {\alpha _2}\Delta {t_2})$$

Let coefficient of surface expansion is $$\beta$$

Area = length $$ \times $$ breadth

$$ = {l_0}(1 + {\alpha _1}\Delta t) \times {b_0}(1 + {\alpha _2}\Delta t)$$

$$ = {l_0}{b_0}(1 + {\alpha _1}\Delta t)(1 + {\alpha _2}\Delta t)$$

$$ = {S_0}(1 + {\alpha _1}\Delta t + {\alpha _2}\Delta t + ...)$$

where, $${S_0} = {l_0}.\,{b_0}$$

= Initial area of surface

In state of expansion,

$${S_t} = {L_t} \times {B_t}$$

$$ = {l_0}{b_0}(1 + {\alpha _1}\Delta t)(1 + {\alpha _2}\Delta t)$$

$$ = {S_0}(1 + {\alpha _1}\Delta t + {\alpha _2}\Delta t + ...)$$

$${S_t} = {S_0}(1 + \beta \Delta t)$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${S_0}(1 + \beta \Delta t) = {S_0}(1 + {\alpha _1}\Delta t + {\alpha _2}\Delta t + ...)$$

$$\beta .\,\Delta t = {\alpha _1}\Delta t + {\alpha _2}\Delta t$$

$$\beta = {\alpha _1} + {\alpha _2}$$

content: A gas bubble of 2 cm diameter rises through a liquid of density 1.75 g cm<sup>$$-$$3</sup> with a fixed speed of 0.35 cms<sup>$$-$$1</sup>. Neglect the density of the gas. The coefficient of viscosity of the liquid is
explanation:
The gas bubble is rising through the liquid of density 175 g/cm3 with constant speed. The acting forces on bubble are



Buoyancy force of liquid = Fb (in upward direction)

Viscous force due to liquid = Fv (in downward direction)

$$ \therefore $$ Fv = Fb

For free falling in a viscous medium

Fv = 6 $$\pi$$$$\eta $$rvT

$$\eta $$ = viscosity coefficient of the liquid

r = radius of bubble

Fb = mg = v $$ \times $$ $$\rho$$ . g

= $${4 \over 3}$$$$\pi$$r3$$\rho$$e . g

6$$\pi$$$$\eta $$rvt = $${4 \over 3}$$ $$\pi$$r3$$\rho$$e . g

$$\eta $$ = $${{24\pi .{r^3}{\rho _e}.g} \over {3 \times 6\pi r{v_T}}} = {2 \over 9}.{{{r^2}{\rho _e}.g} \over {{v_T}}}$$

here, D = 2 cm, r = 1 cm = 1 $$ \times $$ 10$$-$$2 m

$$\rho$$e = 1.75 g/cm3 = 1.75 $$ \times $$ 10+3 kg/m3

g = 10 m/s2

vT = terminal velocity

= 0.35 $$ \times $$ 10$$-$$2 m/s

$$\eta = {2 \over 9} \times {{{{({{10}^{ - 2}})}^2} \times 1.75 \times {{10}^{ + 3}} \times 10} \over {0.35 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}$$

$$ = {2 \over 9} \times {{{{10}^{ - 4}} \times 1.75 \times {{10}^3} \times 10} \over {0.35 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}$$

$$ = {{2 \times 175 \times 10 \times 10} \over {9 \times 35}} \times 10$$ poise

$$ \simeq 1089$$ poise

content: 1000 droplets of water having 2 mm diameter each coalesce to form a single drop. Given the surface tension of water is 0.072 Nm<sup>$$-$$1</sup>. The energy loss in the process is
explanation:
Let the radius of single drop = t

Radius of small drop = $${{2mm} \over 2}$$

= 1 mm

= 1 $$ \times $$ 10$$-$$3 m

Surface tension of water = 0.072 N/m

The volume of large drop must be equal volume of all small drops.

$${4 \over 3}$$$$\pi$$R3 = 1000$$\left( {{4 \over 3}\pi {r^3}} \right)$$

$$ \therefore $$ R = (1000)1/3 . r = 10r

The initial potential energy of drops system Ui = s $$ \times $$ n $$ \times $$ 4$$\pi$$r2

= s $$ \times $$ 1000 $$ \times $$ 4$$\pi$$r2

= 1000 s . 4$$\pi$$r2

When the droplets coalesce, the final potential energy of the system Uf = s . 4$$\pi$$R2

= s . 4$$\pi$$ $$ \times $$ 100 r2

Energy loss in the formation of large drop

$$\Delta$$E = Uf $$-$$ Ui

= s . 4$$\pi$$100r2 $$-$$ 1000s . 4$$\pi$$r2

= s . $$\pi$$r2 (400 $$-$$ 4000)

= $$-$$ 3600s . $$\pi$$r2

= $$-$$ 3600 $$ \times $$ 0.72 $$ \times $$ 3.14 $$ \times $$ (1 $$ \times $$ 10$$-$$3)2

From catenation

= 813.888 $$ \times $$ 10$$-$$6

= 8.1388 $$ \times $$ 10$$-$$4 J

= 8.146 $$ \times $$ 10$$-$$4 J

content: A wire of length 5 m is stretched by 1 mm by a force of 1 N. What is the value of energy stored in the wire?
explanation:
Energy stored $$ = {1 \over 2}$$ $$\times$$ Force $$\times$$ Extension

$$ = {1 \over 2}$$ $$\times$$ 1 $$\times$$ 0.001 = 0.005 Joule or 0.5 mJ.

content: Two wires A and B of same length are made of same material. Load (F) vs. elongation (x) graph for these two wires is shown in the figure. Then which of the following statement(s) is/are true?

options.content: The cross-section area of A is greater than that of B.
Young's modulus of A is greater than Young's modulus of B.
The cross-sectional area of B is greater than that of A.
Young's modulus of both A and B are same.

explanation:
Load F can be given as

$$F = \left( {{{AY} \over L}} \right)\Delta x$$

where, A = cross-sectional area

Y = Young's modulus

L = length

$$\Delta$$x = elongation in length.

i.e., F versus $$\Delta$$x graph is a straight line of slope $${{YA} \over L}$$.

$${(slope)_A} > {(slope)_B}$$

$${\left( {{{YA} \over L}} \right)_A} > {\left( {{{YA} \over L}} \right)_B}$$

or, $${(A)_A} > {(A)_B}$$

As we know that, they are of same material.

Hence, $${Y_B} = {Y_A}$$

So, option (a) and (d) are correct.

content: A drop of water detaches itself from the exit of a tap when ($$\sigma$$ = surface tension of water, $$\rho$$ = density of water, R = radius of the tap exit, r = radius of the drop)
explanation:
Let mass of the drop = m

Weight of the drop = mg

It will act downward.

The force due to surface tension on the drop = $$\sigma$$2$$\pi$$R

where, R = radius of tap

It will act in upward direction.

The drop of water will detach when

mg > $$\sigma$$2$$\pi$$R

But, m = v $$ \times $$ $$\rho$$ = $${4 \over 3}$$$$\pi$$r3$$\rho$$g

$$ \therefore $$ $${4 \over 3}$$$$\pi$$r3$$\rho$$g > $$\sigma$$ $$ \times $$ 2$$\pi$$R

$$r > {\left[ {{3 \over 2}{{\sigma .R} \over {\rho g}}} \right]^{1/3}}$$
